story:
In the far north of Sweden, the Enchanted Forest wakes early on Midsummer Eve, when the sun barely sets and the trolls prepare a feast beneath a maypole wrapped in blue and yellow daisies. Paths through the trees glow with wildflowers as everyone, even the smallest children, helps. Most food and treasures are kept high in the branches—safe from curious hands and hungry animals—so trolls climb up and down trunks to fetch what they need: dried mushrooms, blueberries, lingonberries, cloudberries, and jars of last year’s berries brewed with bark and honey. Down by the river, young trolls catch river crayfish to boil with dill. It’s also bath day: children splash and cheat in breath-holding contests by breathing through reeds, while Old Troll Mum wrestles squealing youngsters into the water, washes their tangled hair with crushed herbs, and rubs scented oil into their muddy ears and tails.

By the rocks, Harald, one of the oldest trolls, cleans fish and mutters his ritual spell to each glassy eye: “Either you are in, or you are out of the water—so make up your mind.” Moss grows between his toes; his hair hangs to the river; his laugh is mostly gum. It is a raucous, happy morning—until Troll Mum’s cry slices through the trees: “Vilje! Has anyone seen Vilje?”

She dashes from the river, asking everyone. The twins giggle unhelpfully. Harald offers his in-or-out logic until she snaps. Old Oscar, the sharp-eyed wanderer with a monocle and a soft spot for snails, has not seen the boy either. Tore, who guards the food tree, rallies a search party. They comb the riverbanks and wind through the forest, shouting “Vilje!” while the sun strolls across the sky.

By late afternoon, Troll Mum returns to the water and weeps, feet swollen from running. Harald lifts her up, and they head back toward the maypole—only to find a different kind of trouble. Some of the younger trolls have raided the caches and the elderberry brew. They lie groaning with swollen bellies, moaning apologies. The feast seems ruined; the field is quiet; the maypole waits.

Then Tore hears a voice from above. High in the food tree sits a ruffled, teary Vilje. He clambers down, clutching his stomach. He hadn’t eaten his morning porridge, he explains, and “the tummy trolls are screaming.” He climbed the tree to quiet them, peeking at the food, but hid instead—too scared to take anything—and then got stuck between hunger and fear. Tore scolds him—everyone has worried sick—but Troll Mum pulls Vilje close and doesn’t let go. He is safe. That is enough for now.

Evening glows. Just as despair settles, a troop of young trolls who had been out foraging steps from the trees with their own bounty: fresh mushrooms, blueberries, lingonberries, cloudberries, even a nest of warm goose eggs. Little Doby says others are coming. Oscar appears, still shepherding his harmless parade of snails and beetles like honored guests. Harald follows with more fish.

Sorry, they tell Troll Mum. Let’s start the party now. They spread bark and leaves for plates, and the forest fills again with music and laughter. The lesson lingers: eat your porridge, don’t overindulge, and when something breaks, everyone helps mend it. Under a sun that barely dips, the trolls dance around the maypole after all.
